$$W = \frac{(8 \times 10^5 \times 0.15) - (1 \times 10^5 \times 0.6)}{1.5 - 1}$$

$$W = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5 - 0.6 \times 10^5}{0.5}$$

$$W = \frac{0.6 \times 10^5}{0.5} = 1.2 \times 10^5\ \mathrm{J} = 120\ \mathrm{kJ}$$

Therefore, the work done during this process is $120\ \mathrm{kJ}$. Thus, the correct answer is $120$.
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift

question:
The temperature of a metal strip having coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$ is increased from $T_1$ to $T_2$ resulting in increase of its length by $\Delta L_1$. The temperature is further increased from $T_2$ to $T_3$ such that the increase in its length is $\Delta L_2$.

Given $T_3+T_1=2 T_2$ and $T_2-T_1=\Delta T$, the value of $\Delta L_2$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
solution:
When a metal of length $L$ having coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$ is heated by temperature $\Delta T$, then the change in length is given as $\Delta \mathrm{L}=\mathrm{L} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$

For the first interval of heating $\left(\mathrm{T}_1 \rightarrow \mathrm{~T}_2\right)$ :

The increase in length $\Delta \mathrm{L}_1$ is given by $\Delta \mathrm{L}_1=\mathrm{L}_1 \alpha\left(\mathrm{~T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1\right)$

It is given that $\mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1=\Delta \mathrm{T}$ :

$$ \Delta \mathrm{L}_1=\mathrm{L}_1 \alpha \Delta \mathrm{~T} .....(i) $$

The new length at temperature $\mathrm{T}_2$ is:

$$ \mathrm{L}_2=\mathrm{L}_1+\Delta \mathrm{L}_1=\mathrm{L}_1(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}) ........(ii)$$

For the second interval of heating, ( $\mathrm{T}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{~T}_3$ )

The increase in length is $\Delta \mathrm{L}_2$ depends on the length at the start of this stage $\left(\mathrm{L}_2\right)$ :

$$ \Delta \mathrm{L}_2=\mathrm{L}_2 \alpha\left(\mathrm{~T}_3-\mathrm{T}_2\right) $$

From the given condition $\mathrm{T}_3+\mathrm{T}_1=2 \mathrm{~T}_2$ :

$$ \mathrm{T}_3-\mathrm{T}_2=\mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1 $$

Since $\mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1=\Delta \mathrm{T}$, then $\mathrm{T}_3-\mathrm{T}_2=\Delta \mathrm{T}$.

Substituting this into the $\Delta \mathrm{L}_2$ formula:

$$\Delta \mathrm{L}_2=\mathrm{L}_2 \alpha \Delta \mathrm{~T}.......(iii) $$

Substitute the value of $\mathrm{L}_2$ from equation (ii) into equation (iii) :

$$ \Delta \mathrm{L}_2=\left[\mathrm{L}_1(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T})\right] \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \Delta \mathrm{L}_2=\left(\mathrm{L}_1 \alpha \Delta \mathrm{~T}\right)(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}) $$

From equation (i), we know $\mathrm{L}_1 \alpha \Delta \mathrm{~T}=\Delta \mathrm{L}_1$.

$$ \Delta \mathrm{L}_2=\Delta \mathrm{L}_1(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}) $$

Therefore, the value of $\Delta L_2$ is $\Delta L_1[1+\alpha \Delta T]$.

Thus, the correct option is (D).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift

question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : If the average kinetic energy of $\mathrm{H}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecules, kept in two different sized containers are same, then their temperatures will be same.

Reason R : The r.m.s speed of $\mathrm{H}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecules are same at same temperature.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options:
Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are true and $\mathbf{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$

Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are true but $\mathbf{R}$ is NOT the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$

A is true but $\mathbf{R}$ is false

A is false but $\mathbf{R}$ is true
solution:
According to the kinetic theory of gases, the average translational kinetic energy ( $\mathrm{K}_{\text {avg }}$ ) of a gas molecule is directly proportional to its absolute temperature (T).

$$ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{avg}}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{~T} $$

Where $\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}}$ is the Boltzmann constant.

So, the average kinetic energy depends only on the temperature and not on the mass of the molecule or the size/volume of the container.

Since $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{avg}}$ is the same for both $\mathrm{H}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$, their temperatures (T) must be identical.

Therefore, Assertion A is true.

The root mean square (r. m. s.) speed ( $\mathrm{v}_{\text {rms }}$ ) of a gas molecule is given by the formula:

$$ v_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}} $$

Where R is the universal gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, and M is the molar mass of the gas.

At the same temperature (T), $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}$ is inversely proportional to the square root of the molar mass (M).

Since the molar mass of $\mathrm{H}_2(2 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})$ is different from $\mathrm{O}_2(32 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})$, their r.m.s. speeds will be different even at the same temperature.

Therefore, Reason R is false.

Hence, Assertion A is true, but Reason R is false.

So, the correct option is (C).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift

question: One gas of $n_1$ mole of molecules at temperature $T_1$, volume $V_1$, and pressure $P_1$, and another gas of $n_2$ mole of molecules at temperature $T_2$, volume $V_2$, and pressure $P_2$, are mixed resulting in pressure $P$ and volume $V$ of the mixture. The temperature of the mixture is $\_\_\_\_$ .
options: $\frac{\left(T_2 P_1 V_1+T_1 P_2 V_2\right)}{T_1 T_2 P V}$
solution:
The ideal gas law is given by: $\mathrm{PV}=\mathrm{nRT} \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{PVR}}{\mathrm{T}}$

Where R is the universal gas constant.

For the first gas : $\mathrm{n}_1=\frac{\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1 \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{T}_1}$

For the second gas : $\mathrm{n}_2=\frac{\mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2 \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{T}_2}$

When the gases are mixed, the total number of moles $n$ is the sum of the individual moles :

$$ \mathrm{n}=\mathrm{n}_1+\mathrm{n}_2 $$

Using the ideal gas law for the final mixture (pressure P , volume V , and temperature T ) :

$$ \frac{P V R}{T}=\frac{P_1 V_1 R}{T_1}+\frac{P_2 V_2 R}{T_2} $$

$\begin{gathered}\frac{P V}{T}=\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1}+\frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2} \\ \frac{P V}{T}=\frac{P_1 V_1 T_2+P_2 V_2 T_1}{T_1 T_2} \\ \frac{T}{P V}=\frac{T_1 T_2}{P_1 V_1 T_2+P_2 V_2 T_1} \\ T=\frac{T_1 T_2 P V}{P_1 V_1 T_2+P_2 V_2 T_1}\end{gathered}$

Therefore, the temperature of the mixture of two gases is $\frac{\mathrm{T}_1 \mathrm{~T}_2 \mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1 \mathrm{~T}_2+\mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2 \mathrm{~T}_1}$.

Thus, the correct option is (B).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift

question:
An ideal gas undergoes a process maintaining relation between pressure $(P)$ and $\operatorname{volume}(V)$ as $P=P_{\mathrm{o}}\left(1+\left(\frac{V_{\mathrm{o}}}{V}\right)^2\right)^{-1}$, where $P_{\mathrm{o}}$ and $V_{\mathrm{o}}$ are constants. If two samples $A$ and $B$ (two moles each) with initial volumes $V_{\mathrm{o}}$ and $3 V_{\mathrm{o}}$ respectively undergo above mentioned process and attain same pressure, then the difference at the temperatures of these samples, $T_B-T_A$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .

( $R=$ gas constant)
solution:
From the ideal gas law

$$ \mathrm{PV}=\mathrm{nRT} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~T}=\frac{\mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{nR}} $$

The process for the given gas is $\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{o}}\left(1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{o}}}{\mathrm{V}}\right)^2\right)^{-1}$

Substituting the given expression for P :

$$ P=\frac{P_0}{1+\frac{V_0^2}{V^2}}=\frac{P_0 V^2}{V^2+V_0^2} $$

Now, substituting this P into the temperature equation :

$$ T=\frac{1}{n R}\left(\frac{P_0 V^2}{V^2+V_0^2}\right) V=\frac{P_0 V^3}{n R\left(V^2+V_0^2\right)} $$

For sample A , the volume is $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{V}_0$ and the number of moles $\mathrm{n}=2$.

$$ T_A=\frac{P_0\left(V_0\right)^3}{2 R\left(V_0^2+V_0^2\right)}=\frac{P_0 V_0^3}{2 R\left(2 V_0^2\right)}=\frac{P_0 V_0}{4 R} $$

For sample B , the volume is $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=3 \mathrm{~V}_0$ and $\mathrm{n}=2$.

$$ T_B=\frac{P_0\left(3 V_0\right)^3}{2 R\left(\left(3 V_0\right)^2+V_0^2\right)}=\frac{P_0\left(27 V_0^3\right)}{2 R\left(9 V_0^2+V_0^2\right)}=\frac{27 P_0 V_0^3}{2 R\left(10 V_0^2\right)}=\frac{27 P_0 V_0}{20 R} $$

So, the temperature difference is,

$$ T_B-T_A=\frac{27 P_0 V_0}{20 R}-\frac{P_0 V_0}{4 R} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{27 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0-5 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0}{20 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{22 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0}{20 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{11 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0}{10 \mathrm{R}} $$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift

question: An ideal gas at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$ is expanding such that $P T^3=$ constant. The coefficient of volume expansion of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ .
solution:
The coefficient of volume expansion $(\gamma)$ is defined as the fractional change in volume per unit change in temperature at constant pressure :

$$ \gamma=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~V}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dT}}\right) $$

The relation between pressure and temperature is,

$$ \mathrm{PT}^3=\mathrm{C} \text { (where } \mathrm{C} \text { is a constant) } $$

For an ideal gas, $\mathrm{PV}=\mathrm{nRT} \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{nRT}}{\mathrm{V}}$

Substitute P into the given process equation :

$$ \left(\frac{\mathrm{nRT}}{\mathrm{~V}}\right) \mathrm{T}^3=\mathrm{C} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{\mathrm{nRT}^4}{\mathrm{~V}}=\mathrm{C}$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{nR}}{\mathrm{C}}\right) \mathrm{T}^4 $$

Let $\mathrm{k}=\frac{\mathrm{nR}}{\mathrm{C}}$ (another constant), then:

$$ \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{kT}^4 $$

On differentiating $V$ with respect to $T$ :

$$ \frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dT}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dT}}\left(\mathrm{kT}^4\right)=4 \mathrm{kT}^3 $$

Substituting the expressions for V and $\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dT}}$ into the definition of $\gamma$ :

$$ \gamma=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~V}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dT}}\right) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\gamma=\frac{1}{\mathrm{kT}^4}\left(4 \mathrm{kT}^3\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\gamma=\frac{4 \mathrm{kT}^3}{\mathrm{kT}^4}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \gamma=\frac{4}{\mathrm{~T}} $$

Therefore, the coefficient of volume expansion of the gas is $\frac{4}{T}$.

Hence, the correct option is (C).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift

question:
The heat extracted out of $x$ gram of water initially at $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $\operatorname{cool}$ it down to $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is sufficient to evaporate $(1000-x)$ gram of water also initially at $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The value of $x$ (closest integer) is $\_\_\_\_$ .

(Take latent heat of water $2256 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{kg} . \mathrm{K}$, specific heat capacity of water $4200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} . \mathrm{K}$ )
solution:
When x grams of water cools from $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the heat released is:

$$ \mathrm{Q}_1=\mathrm{m}_1 \cdot \mathrm{c} \cdot \Delta \mathrm{~T} $$

Where, $\mathrm{m}_1=\mathrm{xg}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{1000} \mathrm{~kg}$ is the mass of water, $\mathrm{c}=4200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} \cdot \mathrm{K}$ is the specific heat capacity of water, and $\Delta \mathrm{T}=50-0=50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Putting the values,

$$ Q_1=\left(\frac{x}{1000}\right) \times 4200 \times 50 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ Q_1=x \times 4.2 \times 50=210 x J $$

The water to be evaporated is initially at $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. To evaporate it, it must first be heated to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and then converted to steam.

Let $\mathrm{m}_2=(1000-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{g}=\frac{1000-\mathrm{x}}{1000} \mathrm{~kg}$.

Heating from $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$$ \mathrm{Q}_{2 \mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{m}_2 \cdot \mathrm{c} \cdot(100-50) $$

$$ Q_{2 a}=\left(\frac{1000-x}{1000}\right) \times 4200 \times 50=(1000-x) \times 210 \mathrm{~J} $$

Latent heat of vaporization at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$$ \mathrm{Q}_{2 \mathrm{~b}}=\mathrm{m}_2 \cdot \mathrm{~L} $$

Where the latent heat of water is $\mathrm{L}=2256 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{kg}=2,256,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}$.

$$ Q_{2 b}=\left(\frac{1000-x}{1000}\right) \times 2,256,000=(1000-x) \times 2256 J $$

So, the total heat for evaporation is

$$ Q_2=Q_{2 a}+Q_{2 b}=(1000-x) \times[210+2256]=(1000-x) \times 2466 J $$

Using conservation of heat energy, the total amount of heat gained is equal to total amount of heat rejected.

$$ \mathrm{Q}_1=\mathrm{Q}_2 $$

$$ \begin{gathered} 210 \mathrm{x}=(1000-\mathrm{x}) 2466 \\ 210 \mathrm{x}=2,466,000-2466 \mathrm{x} \\ 210 \mathrm{x}+2466 \mathrm{x}=2,466,000 \\ 2676 \mathrm{x}=2,466,000 \\ \mathrm{x}=\frac{2,466,000}{2676} \approx 921.52 \end{gathered} $$

Therefore, the value of x (closest integer) is 922 .
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift

question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason $\mathbf{R}$

Statement I : Change in internal energy of a system containing $n$ mole of ideal gas can be written as $\Delta \mathrm{U}=n \mathrm{C}_v\left(T_{\mathrm{f}}-T_i\right)=\frac{n R}{\gamma-1}\left(T_{\mathrm{f}}-T_i\right)$, where $\gamma=\frac{C_p}{C_v}, T_i=$ initial temperature, $T_{\mathrm{f}}=$ final temperature.

Statement II : Relation between degree of freedom $f$ and $\gamma\left(=C_p / C_v\right)$ is $\left(\gamma=1+\frac{2}{f}\right)$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options:
Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are true and $\mathbf{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$

Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are true but $\mathbf{R}$ is NOT the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$

A is true but $\mathbf{R}$ is false

A is false but $\mathbf{R}$ is true
solution:
Internal energy ( $U$ ) of an ideal gas depends only on temperature. The change in internal energy for any process is given by:

$$ \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{~T}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}}\left(\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{i}}\right) $$

According to Mayer's relation, $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}-\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{R}$.

$$ \frac{C_p}{C_v}-1=\frac{R}{C_v} $$

Since $\gamma=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}}$, then $\gamma-1=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\gamma-1}$.

Substituting $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}$ back into the $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ equation:

$$ \Delta \mathrm{U}=\frac{\mathrm{nR}}{\gamma-1}\left(\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{i}}\right) $$

Thus, Statement I is true.

From the law of equipartition of energy, the molar specific heat at constant volume is $C_v=\frac{f}{2} R$, where $f$ is the degree of freedom.

Using $C_p=C_v+R=\frac{f}{2} R+R=R\left(1+\frac{f}{2}\right)$.

The ratio $\gamma=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{f}}{2}\right)}{(\mathrm{f} / 2) \mathrm{R}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{f} / 2}{\mathrm{f} / 2}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{f}}+1$.

So, $\gamma=1+2$ f.

Thus, Statement II is true.

Statement II is the definition of $\gamma$ in terms of degrees of freedom, but Statement I is based on Mayer's relation which is the definition of $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}$, i.e., not directly from the degrees of freedom.

Therefore, both A and R are true but R is the correct explanation of A .

Thus, the correct option is (B).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift

question:
Consider the following statements:

A. Zeroth law of thermodynamics gives concept of temperature

B. First law of thermodynamics gives concept of internal energy

C. In isothermal expansion of ideal gas, $\Delta Q \neq \Delta W$

D. Product of intensive and extensive variables is extensive

E. The ratio of any extensive variable to mass will be an extensive variable

Choose the correct combination of statements from the options given below:
options:
C, D and E Only

A, B and C Only

A, B and D Only

B, C and D Only
solution:
The Zeroth Law states that if two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other, i.e., the Zeroth law gives idea about the property of temperature to be measured. Hence, statement A is true.

The First Law is the mathematical relation between heat ( $\Delta \mathrm{Q}$ ), change in internal energy ( $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ ) and work done by the system ( $\Delta \mathrm{W}$ )

$$ \Delta \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta \mathrm{W} $$

This relation introduces internal energy ( $U$ ) as a state function which is based on energy conservation. Hence, statement B is true.

In an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, temperature remains constant, so $\Delta U=0$.

From the First Law: $\Delta \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta \mathrm{W} \Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{Q}=0+\Delta \mathrm{W} \Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{W}$.

But as per statement $C$, in isothermal process $\Delta Q \neq \Delta W$, so statement $C$ is False.

Intensive variables are the properties which do not depend on size, for example temperature and pressure.

Extensive variables are the properties depend on size, for example volume.

The product of intensive and extensive property $\mathrm{P} \times \mathrm{V}$ represents work or energy, which depends with the size of the system. Therefore, the product is extensive.

When an extensive variable (like volume or heat capacity) is divided by mass, it becomes a specific quantity (like specific volume or specific heat). Specific quantities are intensive because they do not depend on the total mass of the system. Statement E says it remains extensive, so it is False.

Therefore, only A, B, and D are correct. Hence, the correct option is (C).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift

question:
A cylinder with adiabatic walls is closed at both ends and is divided into two compartments by a frictionless adiabatic piston. Ideal gas is filled in both (left and right) the compartments at same $P, V$,

T. Heating is started from left side until pressure changes to $27 \mathrm{P} / 8$. If initial volume of each compartment was 9 litres then the final volume in right-hand side compartment is $\_\_\_\_$ litres. (for this ideal gas $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}} / \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=1.5$ )
solution:
In left compartment heat is added. The gas expands, increasing its pressure and volume.

In right compartment heat is not added because the wall is adiabatic, but the piston moves toward it because the pressure on the left increases. Since the walls and the piston are adiabatic, the gas in the right compartment undergoes an adiabatic compression.

The piston stops moving when the pressure in both compartments is equal. Therefore, the final pressure in the right compartment $\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{R}}\right)$ will also be $\frac{27 \mathrm{P}}{8}$.

For an adiabatic process, the relationship between pressure $(\mathrm{P})$ and volume $(\mathrm{V})$ is:

$$ \mathrm{P} \mathrm{~V}^\gamma=\text { constant } $$

Where $\gamma=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=1.5$.

$$ P_1 V_1^\gamma=P_2 V_2^\gamma $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{V_2}{V_1}=\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1 / \gamma} $$

The initial pressure in right compartment is $\mathrm{P}_1=\mathrm{P}$ and its initial volume is $\mathrm{V}_1=9$ litres

The final pressure in right compartment is $\mathrm{P}_2=\frac{27 \mathrm{P}}{8}$

Substituting these values:

$$ \frac{V_{\text {final }}}{9}=\left(\frac{P}{27 P / 8}\right)^{1 / 1.5} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\text {final }}}{9}=\left(\frac{8}{27}\right)^{2 / 3}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{V_{\text {final }}}{9}=\frac{4}{9}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ V_{\text {final }}=4 \text { litres } $$

Therefore, the final volume in the right-hand side compartment is 4 litres.

Hence, the correct option is (B).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift

question: If 2 mole of an ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $T$, is mixed with 6 mole of another ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $2 T$ then the temperature of mixture is:
solution:
The internal energy $(U)$ of $n$ moles of an ideal gas at temperature $T$ is given by $U=n C_v T$, where $C_v$ is the molar specific heat at constant volume.

For the mixture, total initial internal energy $\mathrm{U}_{\text {initial }}=\mathrm{U}_1+\mathrm{U}_2$

Total final internal energy $\mathrm{U}_{\text {final }}=\left(\mathrm{n}_1+\mathrm{n}_2\right) \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}, \text { mix }} \mathrm{T}_{\text {mix }}$

Since both gases are monoatomic, their molar specific heats are the same,

$$ C_{v 1}=C_{v 2}=C_{v, \operatorname{mix}}=\frac{3}{2} R $$

Therefore, using conservation of energy (total initial internal energy is equal to final internal energy).

$$ \mathrm{n}_1 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}} \mathrm{~T}_1+\mathrm{n}_2 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}} \mathrm{~T}_2=\left(\mathrm{n}_1+\mathrm{n}_2\right) \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}} \mathrm{~T}_{\operatorname{mix}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{mix}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_1 \mathrm{~T}_1+\mathrm{n}_2 \mathrm{~T}_2}{\left(\mathrm{n}_1+\mathrm{n}_2\right)} $$

Moles of first gas is $\mathrm{n}_1=2$

Temperature of first gas is $\mathrm{T}_1=\mathrm{T}$

Moles of second gas $\mathrm{n}_2=6$

Temperature of second gas $\mathrm{T}_2=2 \mathrm{~T}$

On substituting the values into expression for the temperature of mixture,

$$ \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{mix}}=\frac{(2 \times \mathrm{T})+(6 \times 2 \mathrm{~T})}{2+6} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{T}_{\operatorname{mix}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T}+12 \mathrm{~T}}{8}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{mix}}=\frac{14 \mathrm{~T}}{8}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{mix}}=\frac{7}{4} \mathrm{~T} $$

Therefore, the correct option is (D).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift

question: Two closed vessels of same volume are joined through a narrow tube and both vessels are filled with air of pressure 90 kPa and temperature 400 K . Keeping the temperature of one vessel constant at 400 K the second vessel temperature is raised to 500 K . The final pressure in the vessels is $\_\_\_\_$ kPa .
solution:
Using Ideal Gas Law:

PV = nRT

Where P is pressure, V is volume, n is number of moles, R is the gas constant, and T is absolute temperature. Since the system is closed, the total number of moles ( $\mathrm{n}_{\text {total }}$ ) remains constant before and after heating.

Initial State:

Both vessels (each of volume V ) are at $\mathrm{P}_0=90 \mathrm{kPa}$ and $\mathrm{T}_0=400 \mathrm{~K}$.

$$ \mathrm{n}_{\text {total }}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{RT}_0}+\frac{\mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{RT}_0}=\frac{2 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{RT}_0} \ldots (i)$$

Final State: Pressure becomes $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}$ in both vessels. Vessel 1 stays at $\mathrm{T}_1=400 \mathrm{~K}$, and vessel 2 is at $\mathrm{T}_2=500 \mathrm{~K}$.

$$ \mathrm{n}_{\text {total }}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{RT}_1}+\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{RT}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{R}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right) \ldots (ii)$$

From both the equations,

$$ \frac{2 \mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{RT}_0}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{R}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{2 \mathrm{P}_0}{\mathrm{~T}_0}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right) $$

Substituting the values ( $\mathrm{P}_0=90, \mathrm{~T}_0=400, \mathrm{~T}_1=400, \mathrm{~T}_2=500$ ):

$$ 2 \times \frac{90}{400}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}\left(\frac{1}{400}+\frac{1}{500}\right) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{180}{400}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}\left(\frac{5+4}{2000}\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $0.45=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}\left(\frac{9}{2000}\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ P_f=\frac{0.45 \times 2000}{9}=\frac{900}{9}=100 \mathrm{kPa} $$

Therefore, the final pressure of the vessel is 100 kPa . Thus, the correct option is (A).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift

question:
One mole of diatomic gas having rotational modes only is kept in a cylinder with a piston system. The cross-section area of the cylinder is $4 \mathrm{~cm}^2$. The gas is heated slowly to raise the temperature by $1.2^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ during which the piston moves by 25 mm . The amount of heat supplied to the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ J.

(Atmospheric pressure $=100 \mathrm{kPa}, R=8.3 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol} . \mathrm{K}$ ) (Neglect mass of the piston)
solution:
To find the total heat supplied ( Q ), we use the $1^{\text {st }}$ law of thermodynamics :

$$ Q=\Delta U+W $$

Where, $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ is the change in internal energy and W is the work done by the gas.

The internal energy of an ideal gas depends on its degrees of freedom (f). For a diatomic gas, there are 3 translational and 2 rotational degrees of freedom ( $f=5$ ). However, it is given that the gas has rotational modes only, which means we only consider $f=2$.

The formula for internal energy change is :

$$ \Delta U=\frac{n \mathrm{fR} \Delta T}{2} $$

The amount of gas is 1 mole, $\mathrm{n}=1$ mole, the degree of freedom is $\mathrm{f}=2$, the universal gas constant is $\mathrm{R}= \frac{8.3 \mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{~mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K}}$ and the change in temperature of gas is $\Delta \mathrm{T}=1.2^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=1.2 \mathrm{~K}$

$$ \Delta \mathrm{U}=\frac{1 \times 2 \times 8.3 \times 1.2}{2} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \Delta U=8.3 \times 1.2=9.96 \mathrm{~J} $$

Work done by the gas in a piston system is given by:

$$ \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{P} \Delta \mathrm{~V} $$

Since the mass of the piston is neglected and it moves slowly, the internal pressure P equals the atmospheric pressure ( $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{atm}}$ ).

The change in volume $\Delta \mathrm{V}$ is $\Delta \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{A} \times \Delta \mathrm{x}$, where the cross-sectional area is A and displacement of the piston is $\Delta \mathrm{x}$.

The atmospheric pressure is $\mathrm{P}=100 \mathrm{kPa}=10^5 \mathrm{~Pa}$

The area of cross-section is $\mathrm{A}=4 \mathrm{~cm}^2=4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$

The displacement of piston $\mathrm{d}=25 \mathrm{~mm}=25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$

$$ \Delta V=A \times \Delta x=\left(4 \times 10^{-4}\right) \times\left(25 \times 10^{-3}\right)=100 \times 10^{-7}=10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^3 $$

So, the work done by the gas is;

$$ \mathrm{W}=10^5 \times 10^{-5}=1 \mathrm{~J} $$

Using $1^{\text {st }}$ law of thermodynamics,

$$ \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\mathrm{W} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ Q=9.96 \mathrm{~J}+1 \mathrm{~J}=10.96 \mathrm{~J} $$

Therefore, the amount of heat supplied by the gas is 10.96 J.
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift

question: Initial pressure and volume of a monoatomic ideal gas are $P$ and $V$. The change in internal energy of this gas in adiabatic expansion to volume $V_{\text {final }}=27 \mathrm{~V}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
solution:
In an adiabatic process, no heat is exchanged with the surroundings ( $\mathrm{Q}=0$ ).

For an ideal gas undergoing an adiabatic change, the relationship between pressure and volume is :

$$ \mathrm{PV}^\gamma=\text { constant } $$

Where $\gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats $\left(\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}}\right)$.

For a monoatomic gas, $\gamma=53$.

The initial pressure of gas is $\mathrm{P}_1=\mathrm{P}$.

The initial volume of gas is $\mathrm{V}_1=\mathrm{V}$

The final volume of gas is $V_2=27 \mathrm{~V}$

Using the adiabatic relation $\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1^\gamma=\mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2^\gamma$ :

$$ \mathrm{P} \cdot \mathrm{~V}^{5 / 3}=\mathrm{P}_{\text {final }} \cdot(27 \mathrm{~V})^{5 / 3} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{P}_{\text {final }}=\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{27 \mathrm{~V}}\right)^{5 / 3}=\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{1}{27}\right)^{5 / 3}$

$$ \mathrm{P}_{\text {final }}=\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{1}{3^3}\right)^{5 / 3}=\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^5=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{243} $$

From the $1^{\text {st }}$ law of thermodynamics $(Q=\Delta U+W)$, since $Q=0$, we have:

$$ \Delta U=-W $$

The work done ( W ) in an adiabatic process is:

$$ \mathrm{W}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1-\mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2}{\gamma-1} $$

Therefore:

$$ \Delta U=\frac{P_2 V_2-P_1 V_1}{\gamma-1} $$

Substitute the given values, $\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1=\mathrm{PV}, \mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2=\left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{243}\right) \times(27 \mathrm{~V})=\frac{\mathrm{PV}}{9}$

$\Delta U=\frac{\frac{P V}{9}-P V}{\frac{5}{3}-1}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\Delta U=\frac{-\frac{8}{9} P V}{2 / 3}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\Delta \mathrm{U}=-\frac{8}{9} \mathrm{PV} \times \frac{3}{2}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \Delta \mathrm{U}=-\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{PV} $$

Therefore, the change in internal energy is $-\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{PV}$.

Hence, the correct option is (C).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift
